title: Interpolation and Density Estimation
text:
The air quality dataset (luftqualitaet.gpkg) contains measurements of nitrogen dioxide (NO2) from 2015 for 97 monitoring sites in Switzerland. Nitrogen dioxide is produced when fuels and combustibles are burned, especially at high combustion temperatures, with road traffic being the main source.




library(sf)
library(terra)
library(tmap)
library(gstat)
library(tidyverse)
library(spatstat)

I am using the sf GeoPackage parser to read in the data. For Exercise 1 I also need a polygon that delimits the Swiss national territory. This can be obtained from the swissBOUNDARIES3D dataset, so I am reading it in as well. The CRS needs to be set explicitly for the swissBOUNDARIES3D data; luckily it is already provided in the air quality data, so I do not have to look up any EPSG codes.

air_quality <- sf::st_read("data/luftqualitaet.gpkg", quiet = TRUE)

sf::st_crs(air_quality) <- 2056

swiss_polygon <- sf::st_read(
    "data/swissBOUNDARIES3D_1_5_LV95_LN02.gdb",
    layer = "TLM_LANDESGEBIET", quiet = TRUE
  ) %>%
  st_transform(crs = st_crs(air_quality))

Let’s inspect the data. I am using tmap in interactive mode, as it provides a convenient dashboard for exploration.

tmap_mode("view")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"
ℹ toggle with `tmap::ttm()`

tm_shape(air_quality) +
    tm_polygons()





As expected, the dataset consists of point locations with associated NO2 values.





First, a grid needs to be created with sf::st_make_grid. The cellsize argument defines the edge length of each square grid cell in map units (here metres), so cellsize = 5000 produces a grid of 5000 m x 5000 m squares. Setting what = "centers" returns the centroid of each cell as the prediction location. Smaller cell sizes yield a finer resolution but are more expensive to compute. I use sf::st_filter to remove points that do not lie within Switzerland, because sf::st_make_grid produces a rectangular grid that would otherwise include regions of neighbouring countries.

grid <- sf::st_make_grid(
    swiss_polygon,
    cellsize = 5000,
    what = "centers"
  ) %>%
  sf::st_as_sf(crs = sf::st_crs(air_quality)) %>%
  sf::st_filter(swiss_polygon, .predicate = st_within)

Let’s verify that the grid looks correct:

tmap_mode("plot")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"

tm_shape(grid) +
    tm_dots() +
    tm_title("Prediction Grid (5 km x 5 km, centres)")








All interpolation points lie within Switzerland. Now let’s perform the IDW interpolation.




idw_result <- gstat::idw(
    formula = value ~ 1,
    newdata = grid,
    locations = air_quality,
    idp = 2
)

[inverse distance weighted interpolation]





Converting the interpolated grid to a raster can be done using terra. I first create a data frame where each row represents an interpolated point with three columns: the X and Y coordinates, and the interpolated NO2 concentration.

preds <- idw_result %>%
    sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%
    select(var1.pred) %>%
    as_tibble()

prediction_df <- idw_result %>%
    st_coordinates() %>%
    as_tibble() %>%
    mutate(interpolated_values = preds) %>%
    unpack(interpolated_values)

prediction_df %>% head()

# A tibble: 6 × 3
         X        Y var1.pred
     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
1 2722910. 1077768.      38.0
2 2572910. 1082768.      22.0
3 2577910. 1082768.      22.1
4 2582910. 1082768.      22.3
5 2717910. 1082768.      42.9
6 2722910. 1082768.      40.0


Then terra::rast can create a raster from the data frame using type = "xyz". This tells terra that the first two columns are coordinates and the third column holds the attribute value.

interpolation_raster <- prediction_df %>%
    terra::rast(type = "xyz")


tmap_mode("plot")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"

tm_shape(interpolation_raster) +
  tm_raster(
    col = "var1.pred",
    col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
    col.legend = tm_legend(title = "NO2 (ug/m3)")
  ) +
  tm_title("IDW Interpolation of NO2 Concentrations (idp = 2, nmax = Inf)")

[plot mode] fit legend/component: Some legend items or map compoments do not
fit well, and are therefore rescaled.
ℹ Set the tmap option `component.autoscale = FALSE` to disable rescaling.












The exercise asks to vary idp, nmax/nmin, and maxdist. Showing the interactions for three different parameters simultaneously is very convoluted, so below I discuss the effect of the parameters for a specific case where I vary idp and nmax. The facet plot shows the result of IDW interpolations for the parameters idp in {1, 2, 5} and nmax in {10, 30, Inf} (where Inf means all stations contribute). Each result is rasterised and displayed in a 3 x 3 facet grid.

idp_values  <- c(1, 2, 5)
nmax_values <- c(10, 30, Inf)

param_grid <- expand.grid(idp = idp_values, nmax = nmax_values)

idw_to_raster <- function(idp, nmax, locations, newdata) {
  nmax_arg <- if (is.infinite(nmax)) Inf else nmax
  result <- gstat::idw(
    formula   = value ~ 1,
    locations = locations,
    newdata   = newdata,
    idp       = idp,
    nmax      = nmax_arg
  )
  coords <- st_coordinates(result)
  vals   <- result$var1.pred
  df     <- data.frame(X = coords[, 1], Y = coords[, 2], value = vals)
  terra::rast(df, type = "xyz", crs = st_crs(air_quality)$wkt)
}

raster_list <- purrr::pmap(param_grid, function(idp, nmax) {
  idw_to_raster(idp, nmax, air_quality, grid)
})

[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]

tmap_mode("plot")

map_list <- purrr::pmap(
  list(raster_list, param_grid$idp, param_grid$nmax),
  function(r, idp, nmax) {
    nmax_label <- if (is.infinite(nmax)) "all" else as.character(nmax)
    tm_shape(r) +
      tm_raster(
        col = "value",
        col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
        col.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
      ) +
      tm_title(text = paste0("idp=", idp, ", nmax=", nmax_label))
  }
)

tmap_arrange(maps = map_list, ncol = 3, nrow = 3)











Effect of idp (read row-wise): The pattern is more blurred for low idp values and becomes increasingly varied and distinct for higher values. With a low idp, a measurement point still contributes meaningfully to distant interpolation points, so two neighbouring interpolated points are influenced by largely the same set of stations and therefore take on similar values. As idp increases, the influence of a measurement drops off much more quickly with distance. Consequently, two neighbouring interpolated points may be influenced by mostly different stations, producing sharper contrasts and a less “smeared out” surface.
Effect of nmax (read column-wise): The first row provides a clear example. For larger values of nmax, the NO2 concentration surface becomes blurrier because each interpolation point is influenced by more and more measurement stations. As the set of contributing stations grows, neighbouring interpolation points share an increasingly large common influence, making them more similar.
Extreme cases: The lower-left panel (idp = 1, nmax = all) combines a slow weighting decay with the maximum number of contributing stations, producing a very blurry result. Conversely, the upper-right panel (idp = 5, nmax = 10) combines a fast decay with few contributing stations, yielding very sharp boundaries between zones of different values.




Another simple interpolation option is the nearest-neighbour approach, which can be implemented using Voronoi polygons. The resulting sfc object is converted to sf using st_as_sf, and then st_join is used to attach the measured NO2 values to the polygons.



voronoi <- air_quality %>%
    sf::st_geometry() %>%
    sf::st_union() %>%
    sf::st_voronoi() %>%
    sf::st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>%
    sf::st_as_sf() %>%
    sf::st_intersection(swiss_polygon) %>%
    sf::st_join(air_quality)

tm_shape(voronoi) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "value",
      fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "NO2 (ug/m3)")
    ) +
    tm_title("Nearest-Neighbour NO2 Interpolation (Voronoi)") +
    tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE, legend.outside.position = "right")











This example illustrates several shortcomings of Voronoi-based interpolation:

Discontinuity. The interpolation is not continuous. For NO2 concentration this is unrealistic, because the concentration of a gas in the atmosphere is a continuous phenomenon, not a discretely changing one.
Constant regions. Large regions are assigned the exact same NO2 value. This is also unrealistic, since we can assume that gas concentrations decrease with distance from the emission source. Having patches of identical values does not correctly model atmospheric behaviour.
Edge effects. At the edges of the measurement area, the Voronoi polygons become very large. To mitigate this, it is recommended to also sample points in a buffer zone around the area of interest.

The plot below shows both the clipped and unclipped Voronoi tessellations side by side, making the large-polygon edge effect visible.

tmap_mode("plot")

voronoi_unclipped <- air_quality %>%
    sf::st_geometry() %>%
    sf::st_union() %>%
    sf::st_voronoi() %>%
    sf::st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>%
    sf::st_as_sf() %>%
    sf::st_join(air_quality)

map_clipped <- tm_shape(voronoi) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "value",
      fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
    tm_title("Clipped to Switzerland")

map_unclipped <- tm_shape(voronoi_unclipped) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "value",
      fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
    tm_title("Unclipped (raw tessellation)")

tmap_arrange(map_clipped, map_unclipped, ncol = 2)








The unclipped version makes clear how the Voronoi polygons extend very far at the edges of the measurement network – the border stations each “own” a huge territory that stretches towards infinity (here truncated by the bounding box). This is the edge effect mentioned in point 3 above.

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation
text: The air quality dataset (luftqualitaet.gpkg) contains measurements of nitrogen dioxide (NO2) from 2015 for 97 monitoring sites in Switzerland. Nitrogen dioxide is produced when fuels and combustibles are burned, especially at high combustion temperatures, with road traffic being the main source.

library(sf)

library(tmap)
library(gstat)
library(tidyverse)
library(spatstat)

    tm_polygons()





As expected, the dataset consists of point locations with associated NO2 values.

prediction_df %>% head()

1 2722910. 1077768.      38.0
2 2572910. 1082768.      22.0
3 2577910. 1082768.      22.1

tmap_mode("plot")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"

tm_shape(interpolation_raster) +

    col = "var1.pred",

[plot mode] fit legend/component: Some legend items or map compoments do not
fit well, and are therefore rescaled.
ℹ Set the tmap option `component.autoscale = FALSE` to disable rescaling.

Showing the interactions for three different parameters simultaneously is very convoluted, so below I discuss the effect of the parameters for a specific case where I vary idp and nmax. nmax_values <- c(10, 30, Inf)

[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]

tmap_mode("plot")

map_list <- purrr::pmap(
  list(raster_list, param_grid$idp, param_grid$nmax),
  function(r, idp, nmax) {

)

tmap_arrange(maps = map_list, ncol = 3, nrow = 3)

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation
text:
Another simple interpolation option is the nearest-neighbour approach, which can be implemented using Voronoi polygons. The resulting sfc object is converted to sf using st_as_sf, and then st_join is used to attach the measured NO2 values to the polygons.



voronoi <- air_quality %>%
    sf::st_geometry() %>%
    sf::st_union() %>%
    sf::st_voronoi() %>%
    sf::st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>%
    sf::st_as_sf() %>%
    sf::st_intersection(swiss_polygon) %>%
    sf::st_join(air_quality)

tm_shape(voronoi) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "value",
      fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "NO2 (ug/m3)")
    ) +
    tm_title("Nearest-Neighbour NO2 Interpolation (Voronoi)") +
    tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE, legend.outside.position = "right")











This example illustrates several shortcomings of Voronoi-based interpolation:

Discontinuity. The interpolation is not continuous. For NO2 concentration this is unrealistic, because the concentration of a gas in the atmosphere is a continuous phenomenon, not a discretely changing one.
Constant regions. Large regions are assigned the exact same NO2 value. This is also unrealistic, since we can assume that gas concentrations decrease with distance from the emission source. Having patches of identical values does not correctly model atmospheric behaviour.
Edge effects. At the edges of the measurement area, the Voronoi polygons become very large. To mitigate this, it is recommended to also sample points in a buffer zone around the area of interest.

The plot below shows both the clipped and unclipped Voronoi tessellations side by side, making the large-polygon edge effect visible.

tmap_mode("plot")

voronoi_unclipped <- air_quality %>%
    sf::st_geometry() %>%
    sf::st_union() %>%
    sf::st_voronoi() %>%
    sf::st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>%
    sf::st_as_sf() %>%
    sf::st_join(air_quality)

map_clipped <- tm_shape(voronoi) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "value",
      fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
    tm_title("Clipped to Switzerland")

map_unclipped <- tm_shape(voronoi_unclipped) +
    tm_polygons(
      fill = "value",
      fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      fill.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
    tm_title("Unclipped (raw tessellation)")

tmap_arrange(map_clipped, map_unclipped, ncol = 2)








The unclipped version makes clear how the Voronoi polygons extend very far at the edges of the measurement network – the border stations each “own” a huge territory that stretches towards infinity (here truncated by the bounding box). This is the edge effect mentioned in point 3 above.

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation
text:
Data
The dataset rotmilan.gpkg originates from a larger research project of the Sempach Ornithological Institute, accessible via the Movebank platform (see Scherler 2020). It tracks a single red kite individual fitted with a transmitter since 2017 that travels across Central Europe. In this exercise, we only work with the data points recorded in Switzerland.

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation
text:
Exercise 1: Kernel Density Estimation
To calculate a 2D kernel density over the red kite data, we use the density function from the spatstat package. The point pattern is converted to class ppp using as.ppp. We then compare four automatic bandwidth selectors: bw.diggle, bw.CvL, bw.scott, and bw.ppl.

Reading in the Red Kite Data

rotmilan <- sf::st_read("data/rotmilan.gpkg", quiet = TRUE)



Solution
When running a Gaussian kernel, sigma is the standard deviation of the kernel. As discussed in the lecture, choosing a reasonable kernel bandwidth is often challenging and subjective. One generally wants a bandwidth that is neither too large (blurring everything into one blob) nor too small (causing individual occurrences to produce sharp spikes). I start by trying out several sigma values manually, and then pick the one that best balances these criteria. To inform this choice, it is advisable to first look at the underlying point distribution.

tmap_mode("plot")

tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
tm_shape(rotmilan) +
    tm_dots(col = "firebrick", size = 0.03, alpha = 0.4) +
    tm_title("Red Kite GPS Observations in Switzerland") +
    tm_layout(legend.show = FALSE)










Manual Bandwidth Selection

rotmilan_vect <- rotmilan %>%
  terra::vect()
swiss_owin <- swiss_polygon %>%
  terra::vect() %>%
  terra::aggregate() %>%
  sf::st_as_sf() %>%
  sf::st_cast("POLYGON") %>%
  spatstat.geom::as.owin()
rotmilan_ppp  <- as.ppp(rotmilan)
Window(rotmilan_ppp) <- swiss_owin
swiss_vect    <- terra::vect(swiss_polygon)
sigma_values  <- c(500, 4000, 8000, 16000, 30000)
sigma_rasters <- lapply(sigma_values, function(s) {
  density(rotmilan_ppp, sigma = s, kernel = "gaussian", eps = 1000) %>%
    terra::rast() %>%
    terra::crop(swiss_vect) %>%
    terra::mask(swiss_vect)
})
tmap_mode("plot")
sigma_maps <- lapply(seq_along(sigma_values), function(i) {
  tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
    tm_shape(sigma_rasters[[i]]) +
    tm_raster(
      col = names(sigma_rasters[[i]]),
      col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      col.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_title(text = paste0("sigma = ", sigma_values[i], " m"))
})
tmap_arrange(maps = sigma_maps, ncol = 2, nrow = 3)










Choosing the Best Sigma

sigma = 500 m is too small to pick up any meaningful pattern.
sigma = 4000 m captures the major hotspot of the red kite’s location but misses the rest of the pattern.
sigma = 8000 m reveals a second hotspot in addition to the main one.
sigma = 16 000 m successfully shows one major hotspot and a range of less dense observations nearby.
sigma = 30 000 m is too large and completely glosses over the spatial pattern, leaving just one broad blob.

A sigma of 16 000 m therefore seems most suitable:

tmap_mode("plot")

tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
tm_shape(sigma_rasters[[4]]) +
    tm_raster(
      col = names(sigma_rasters[[4]]),
      col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      col.legend = tm_legend(title = "Density", show = TRUE)
    ) +
    tm_title("KDE of Red Kite Observations (sigma = 16 000 m)") +
    tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE, legend.outside.position = "right")










Automatic Bandwidth Selection
As mentioned above, picking sigma arbitrarily is not ideal because it introduces subjectivity. Instead, several methods can derive sigma values based on the actual point pattern. These methods work differently, but they all fit sigma by optimising some loss function with respect to the data (bw.scott is the exception – it is simply a rule of thumb).

bw_methods <- list(
  "bw.diggle" = bw.diggle,
  "bw.CvL"    = bw.CvL,
  "bw.scott"  = function(x) bw.scott(x, isotropic = TRUE),
  "bw.ppl"    = bw.ppl
)

kde_rasters <- lapply(names(bw_methods), function(name) {
  sigma_val <- bw_methods[[name]](rotmilan_ppp)
  cat(name, ": sigma =", round(as.numeric(sigma_val), 1), "m\n")
  density(rotmilan_ppp, sigma = sigma_val, kernel = "gaussian", eps = 1000) %>%
    terra::rast() %>%
    terra::crop(swiss_vect) %>%
    terra::mask(swiss_vect)
})

bw.diggle : sigma = 54 m


bw.CvL : sigma = 81219.6 m


bw.scott : sigma = 2805.6 m


bw.ppl : sigma = 695.7 m

names(kde_rasters) <- names(bw_methods)

tmap_mode("plot")

kde_maps <- lapply(names(kde_rasters), function(name) {
  tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  tm_shape(kde_rasters[[name]]) +
    tm_raster(
      col = names(kde_rasters[[name]]),
      col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      col.legend = tm_legend(show = FALSE)
    ) +
    tm_title(text = name)
})

tmap_arrange(maps = kde_maps, ncol = 2, nrow = 2)










Interpretation of Automatic Bandwidth Selectors
Interestingly, none of the automated methods produced a truly satisfactory result here:

bw.diggle: Does not pick up any meaningful pattern – the computed sigma was too small.
bw.CvL: Everything is smeared out into a single large blob. The sigma is far too large.
bw.scott: Shows the major hotspot but misses the density created by the remaining observations.
bw.ppl: Only picks up a very faint signal of the densest region; other areas are not visible.

Of these, bw.scott provides the most interpretable result:

tmap_mode("plot")

tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
tm_shape(kde_rasters[["bw.scott"]]) +
    tm_raster(
      col = names(kde_rasters[["bw.scott"]]),
      col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
      col.legend = tm_legend(title = "Density", show = TRUE)
    ) +
    tm_title("KDE of Red Kite Observations (bw.scott bandwidth)") +
    tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE, legend.outside.position = "right")

title: Interpolation and Density Estimation
text:
Exercise 2: Voronoi / Thiessen Polygons (Density)
Thiessen polygons offer an alternative for visualising differences in the density distribution of point data. Smaller polygons indicate areas where observations are denser.

Solution

voronoi_density <- rotmilan %>%
    sf::st_geometry() %>%
    sf::st_union() %>%
    sf::st_voronoi() %>%
    sf::st_collection_extract("POLYGON") %>%
    sf::st_as_sf() %>%
    sf::st_intersection(swiss_polygon)

tmap_mode("view")

tm_shape(swiss_polygon) +
    tm_borders(col = "black", lwd = 1) +
tm_shape(voronoi_density) +
    tm_polygons(fill = "lightblue", col = "grey40", lwd = 0.3) +
    tm_title("Red Kite GPS Observations: Voronoi Density")







Interpretation
Voronoi density visualisations can be read as follows: the denser the tessellation in a given region, the higher the density of the phenomenon in that area. For the red kite data, this approach adequately shows the distribution of the bird’s positions. Subjectively, the Voronoi visualisation communicates the spatial density pattern more effectively than the kernel density estimation in this case. This may be because the automatic bandwidth selectors did not converge on a suitable sigma for this particular dataset, whereas the Voronoi approach requires no parameter tuning at all.


Usage of KI
Claude Sonnet (Anthropic) was used as an aid for this exercise. It was used to review the submitted document, identify and fix inconsistencies in file naming across code chunks, add the required _quarto.yml configuration file. Further, It was used for setting up facet plots of the parameter scans and to perform spell checking and improve formulation and improve formulation of the text passages.
